Inside scaled dot-product attention:
Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKT / √dk)V
dₖ = 64 · Q row 2
Softmax weights on values after dividing query-key dot products by the square root of key dimension.

Parameters
64
1 256
row 2
row 0 row 5

What you're seeing

The selected query forms dot products with all keys. The first row applies softmax; the second divides the dot products by √dk before softmax. As dk grows, the weights on the values become more even. Changing Q shows another row.
Multi-head attention in parallel:
Queries, keys, and values are linearly projected through four heads in parallel and concatenated.
1 / 4
14
0.70
0.201.00

What you're seeing

Queries, keys, and values are linearly projected h times in parallel. Head h_i selects one projected version; increasing α strengthens its lines. The head_1,...,head_h outputs stay concatenated before W^O.
Sine and cosine positional encodings:
Sine and cosine functions at the selected position and dimension.

Inputs

18
0 token 80 token
4
0 channel 15 channel

What you're seeing

Each position selects values from sine and cosine functions. Moving pos changes the selected position; changing i changes the frequency. Together, these values add relative or absolute position information to the input embeddings.
Number of domains and quench time:
Single realization of j_z(x).

Single realization · jz(x)

Nq ∼ τq−0.33
19 number of domains

Parameters

80 ms
10 ms 400 ms
0.33
0.20 0.60

What you're seeing

Longer quench time produces fewer domains according to the selected power law. The scaling exponent sets how quickly the number of domains falls. A single realization shows jz(x); different noise sampling provides a different seed for symmetry breaking domains in each trajectory.
Freezing time and non-adiabatic evolutio:
KZM

t̂

Freezing time 11.0 ms
quench time scale relaxation time non-adiabatic
The quench time scale and relaxation time meet at the freezing time.

Parameters

20 ms 400 ms
1 3
t̂ = (τ₀ τqνz)1/(1+νz)
τ₀ = 1 ms · ν = 0.5

What you're seeing

The quench time scale and relaxation time become equal at ±t̂. The interval between them is non-adiabatic. Changing τq or z moves these points and changes the freezing time.
Lower branch with one or two minima:
E−(k) = k²/2 − √((γk − δ/2)² + (Ω/2)²)
Ω < Ωc · two minima at δ = 0
Lower branch dispersion.

Controls

0.75
0.40 1.50
0.00 Er
−0.80 Er +0.80 Er

What you're seeing

At δ = 0, the lower branch has two degenerate minima below Ωc and one minimum above Ωc. Changing Ω/Ωc shows this change; changing δ changes the displayed dispersion through γk − δ/2.
Nearly identical initial conditions dive:
Two double pendulums with nearly identical initial conditions diverge over time.
Initial conditions reset.

Initial conditions

Moving either value resets both.

118 deg
1.0 deg

What you're seeing

Two double pendulums begin with nearly identical initial conditions, so their trajectories first overlap. The coupled nonlinear Euler–Lagrange equations then carry them apart: a small Difference becomes visible as they diverge. Moving either control resets both.
Anisotropy: 3D Gaussians align with surf:
Full covariance matrix
Single scalar value
Drag or arrow keys
wheel to change view

Parameters

1,00010,000
0.010.50
0.15.0

What you're seeing

Both sides use the same number of points. With a full covariance matrix, anisotropy changes each 3D Gaussian so it can align with surfaces. With a single scalar value, the radius of the 3D Gaussian remains equal on all three axes.
